articles.title: Novel generative AI model enables atomic-scale prediction of protein-protein interactions

articles.title: AI reveals unexpected source of antibiotic candidates in prion proteins
articles.summary: New antibiotic candidates for drug-resistant bacteria may reside inside prions, misfolded proteins in the brain best known for rare and fatal degenerative brain diseases. Prion and prion-like proteins may hide short…

articles.title: DNA time stamps reveal the strawberry’s surprising origins
articles.summary: Researchers have created a new way to reconstruct the evolutionary history of complex plant genomes by analyzing genetic traces left by transposable elements. The technique revealed that modern strawberries were…

articles.title: AI-designed protein unlocks virus-like shells that could reshape vaccine and drug delivery
articles.summary: An international research team led by a Korean scientist has succeeded in designing large-scale protein structures that faithfully replicate the self-assembly principles found in naturally occurring viruses, using…

articles.title: Saturday Citations: Intermittent fasting and chronic stress; macroscopic entanglement; gamma-ray bursts
articles.summary: Researchers reported this week a deadly outbreak of plague in Siberia 5,500 years ago, revealing that Yersinia pestis evolved lethal genetic traits far earlier than suspected. A drug developed for heart tissue repair…

articles.title: A private company will build and launch NASA's next Mars orbiter in 2028 — and it's not SpaceX
articles.summary: A California-based company will design, build and launch a spacecraft for NASA's Aeolus mission to orbit around Mars, where the probe will be the first to provide daily measurements of the planet's global environment.

articles.title: James Webb Space Telescope discovers extreme exoplanet being roasted by its home star
articles.summary: Who ordered the roasted exoplanet? Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope found a world that really puts the "hot" in "Hot Jupiter."

articles.title: 'Best. Mars. Mission. Ever.' Scientists hail MAVEN's legacy as NASA retires Red Planet orbiter
articles.summary: NASA's MAVEN Mars mission ended after 11 years, having revealed how the planet lost its atmosphere and served as a key communications relay for surface missions.

articles.title: Hubble Glimpses Merging Galaxy Clusters
articles.summary: This NASA Hubble Space Telescope image features a galaxy cluster, called CL0016+1609 or MACS J0018.5+1626, that is very bright at X-ray wavelengths and is one of the most extensively studied clusters at X-ray and radio…

articles.title: Remarkable fossils rewrite the story of how animals conquered the land
articles.summary: Palaeontologists have found new evidence that the early ancestors of amphibians, reptiles and mammals did not have a larval stage with external gills like modern frogs or salamanders

articles.title: On the brink of extinction, the vaquita gets a digital lifeline
articles.summary: Scientists have digitally preserved the world’s most endangered marine mammal by creating highly detailed 3D models of a vaquita skeleton using advanced imaging technology. The virtual archive provides an unprecedented…

articles.title: From birds to fish, how extreme heat causes wildlife to suffer
articles.summary: Like humans, wildlife is increasingly vulnerable as climate change fuels longer and more intense heat waves, disrupting feeding and breeding and, in extreme cases, proving fatal.

articles.title: Plant hormones in mammals challenge view of cytokinins as purely botanical
articles.summary: In biology, cytokinins were long considered regulators exclusive to the plant kingdom, where they control, among other things, growth and responses to stress. Until now, little research has been conducted into whether…

articles.title: These bees have nowhere to hide from extreme heat
articles.summary: A major study of Australian native bees found that stem-nesting species may be the first to feel the impact of climate change. Unlike bees that nest underground, they have few ways to escape dangerous heat. Researchers…

articles.title: Tropical Storm Arthur
articles.summary: The first named storm of the 2026 Atlantic hurricane season brought intense rainfall and the threat of flash flooding to the U.S. Gulf Coast.

articles.title: Scientists discover an earthquake gate as California faults reach their highest stress levels in 1,000 years
articles.summary: A new study suggests Southern California's major fault system is more stressed than at any point in the last 1,000 years. Researchers found that the Cajon Pass, where the San Andreas and San Jacinto faults meet, could…

articles.title: Antarctica is offering 30 to 50 years' worth of warning on sea level rise, models suggest
articles.summary: Scientists predict that the next three to five decades provide a critical window to anticipate and plan for Antarctic ice loss and its contribution to sea level rise. Research published in Nature, led by Monash…

articles.title: Midwest tornadoes possible today, flash floods across Gulf?

articles.title: Gas from Uranus reveals it has an icy centre
articles.summary: Carbon monoxide in Uranus's deep atmosphere indicates that the planet contains more ice than rock, suggesting it formed more like Neptune than we thought

articles.title: New Scientist recommends an excellent look at the future of work
articles.summary: Sarah O'Connor's We Are Not Machines explores how we are contorting ourselves to fit AI into our working lives – and what to do about it, finds Tom Knowles

articles.title: Are useful and error-free quantum computers only two years away?
articles.summary: Quantum computing firm QuEra says it plans to make a fault-tolerant quantum computer and offer it to users through the cloud in 2028, which will require a real leap in engineering

articles.title: Technology is changing our perspective on nature – at every scale
articles.summary: Inspired by Ariel Waldman’s docuseries Life Unearthed, columnist Annalee Newitz explores how microscopes, drones and specialised cameras are giving us an unprecedented view of nature from many different vantage points

articles.title: Tiny objects swimming in a superfluid of light move against the flow

articles.title: Could cosmic memory explain dark matter, dark energy, and black holes?
articles.summary: A new theory suggests the universe is constantly recording its own history in the fabric of spacetime. If correct, this cosmic memory could help solve some of the biggest puzzles in physics, from black holes to dark…

articles.title: Belgian Nobel laureate Francois Englert dies aged 93
articles.summary: Belgian scientist Francois Englert, a particle physics specialist who won the Nobel Prize in 2013 for his work on the Higgs boson, has died at 93.

articles.title: Scientists trace high-energy ghost particle to the 'Shadow Blaster' galaxy
articles.summary: "If confirmed, Shadow Blaster would be the first-ever individual dusty star-forming galaxy directly linked to a high-energy neutrino event."

articles.title: Einstein’s “biggest blunder” may finally have an explanation
